from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of Triple objects about a CelestialBody into a fluent sentence.
    Handles multiple triples by joining clause fragments with commas and a final
    conjunction.
    """
    if not triples:
        return ""

    # Simple template map for known predicates; fall back to a generic pattern.
    # Templates avoid adding units that are already present in the object string.
    templates = {
        "apoapsis": "{subject} has an apoapsis of {object}",
        "periapsis": "{subject} has a periapsis of {object}",
        "orbitalPeriod": "{subject} has an orbital period of {object}",
        "epoch": "{subject} was recorded at epoch {object}",
        "discoverer": "{subject} was discovered by {object}",
        "discovered": "{subject} was discovered on {object}",
        "averageSpeed": "{subject} moves at an average speed of {object}",
        "escapeVelocity": "{subject} has an escape velocity of {object}",
        "mass": "{subject} has a mass of {object}",
        "temperature": "{subject} has a temperature of {object}",
        "density": "{subject} has a density of {object}",
        "rotationPeriod": "{subject} has a rotation period of {object}",
        "maximumTemperature": "{subject} reaches a maximum temperature of {object}",
        "meanTemperature": "{subject} has a mean temperature of {object}",
        "minimumTemperature": "{subject} reaches a minimum temperature of {object}",
        "formerName": "{subject} was formerly known as {object}",
        "absoluteMagnitude": "{subject} has an absolute magnitude of {object}",
        "surfaceArea": "{subject} has a surface area of {object}",
        "deathCause": "{subject} died of {object}",
        "deathPlace": "{subject} died in {object}",
        "nationality": "{subject} is {object}",
        "stateOfOrigin": "{subject} originates from {object}",
        "almaMater": "{subject} studied at {object}",
        "birthPlace": "{subject} was born in {object}",
        "doctoralStudent": "{subject} supervised doctoral student {object}",
        "deathDate": "{subject} died on {object}",
        "birthDate": "{subject} was born on {object}",
    }

    # Build a flat list of clause strings for each triple.
    clauses = []
    for triple in triples:
        tmpl = templates.get(triple.predicate,
                             "{subject} {predicate} {object}")
        clause = tmpl.format(subject=triple.subject,
                             predicate=triple.predicate,
                             object=triple.object)
        clauses.append(clause)

    # If there is only one clause, just return it with a period.
    if len(clauses) == 1:
        sentence = clauses[0] + "."
        return sentence[0].upper() + sentence[1:]

    # Otherwise combine all clauses into a single complex sentence.
    # Keep the first clause as‑is, lower‑case the rest and join with commas,
    # using “and” before the final clause.
    first, *rest = clauses
    rest = [c[0].lower() + c[1:] if c else c for c in rest]

    if len(rest) == 1:
        combined = f"{first} and {rest[0]}."
    else:
        combined = f"{first}, " + ", ".join(rest[:-1]) + f", and {rest[-1]}."

    # Capitalize the very first character.
    return combined[0].upper() + combined[1:]

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END

It is a program that converts a list of triples into natural language text. The given triples will be associated with the topic: CelestialBody. The program must implement a function called 'predict' that accepts a list of triples and generates a coherent, contextually relevant sentence that accurately represents the information contained in the triples. Ensure that the generated text is fluent, grammatically correct, and maintains the meaning of the original data. There can be multiple triples given that make up a complex sentence. Converting all those triples into one sentence will award greater score. An example of a complex sentence:
Triples:
(Antwerp | cityServed | Antwerp International Airport)
(Belgium | country | Antwerp)
(City of Brussels | capital | Belgium)
Example sentence:
"Antwerp International Airport serves the city of Antwerp which is in Belgium, where the capital is Brussels."

The 'predict' function returns that sentence as a string. Below is the list of all possible predicates that can be given as an input. With the given predicates and example triples implement the 'predict' function so it can process all the possible predicates:

# Current Program
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of Triple objects about a CelestialBody into a fluent sentence.
    Handles multiple triples by joining clause fragments with commas and a final
    conjunction.
    """
    if not triples:
        return ""

    # Simple template map for known predicates; fall back to a generic pattern.
    # Templates avoid adding units that are already present in the object string.
    templates = {
        "apoapsis": "{subject} has an apoapsis of {object}",
        "periapsis": "{subject} has a periapsis of {object}",
        "orbitalPeriod": "{subject} has an orbital period of {object}",
        "epoch": "{subject} was recorded at epoch {object}",
        "discoverer": "{subject} was discovered by {object}",
        "discovered": "{subject} was discovered on {object}",
        "averageSpeed": "{subject} moves at an average speed of {object}",
        "escapeVelocity": "{subject} has an escape velocity of {object}",
        "mass": "{subject} has a mass of {object}",
        "temperature": "{subject} has a temperature of {object}",
        "density": "{subject} has a density of {object}",
        "rotationPeriod": "{subject} has a rotation period of {object}",
        "maximumTemperature": "{subject} reaches a maximum temperature of {object}",
        "meanTemperature": "{subject} has a mean temperature of {object}",
        "minimumTemperature": "{subject} reaches a minimum temperature of {object}",
        "formerName": "{subject} was formerly known as {object}",
        "absoluteMagnitude": "{subject} has an absolute magnitude of {object}",
        "surfaceArea": "{subject} has a surface area of {object}",
        "deathCause": "{subject} died of {object}",
        "deathPlace": "{subject} died in {object}",
        "nationality": "{subject} is {object}",
        "stateOfOrigin": "{subject} originates from {object}",
        "almaMater": "{subject} studied at {object}",
        "birthPlace": "{subject} was born in {object}",
        "doctoralStudent": "{subject} supervised doctoral student {object}",
        "deathDate": "{subject} died on {object}",
        "birthDate": "{subject} was born on {object}",
    }

    # Group clauses by subject to avoid repeating the subject name.
    subject_map = {}
    for triple in triples:
        tmpl = templates.get(triple.predicate,
                             "{subject} {predicate} {object}")
        clause = tmpl.format(subject=triple.subject,
                             predicate=triple.predicate,
                             object=triple.object)
        subject_map.setdefault(triple.subject, []).append(clause)

    sentences = []
    for subject, clauses in subject_map.items():
        if len(clauses) == 1:
            # Single clause for this subject.
            sentences.append(clauses[0] + ".")
        else:
            # Keep the first clause as‑is, strip the subject from the rest.
            first, *rest = clauses
            # Remove the leading subject from subsequent clauses.
            stripped_rest = []
            for c in rest:
                # Find the first occurrence of the subject and remove it.
                if c.startswith(subject):
                    c = c[len(subject):].lstrip()
                # Lower‑case the first character to make it a continuation.
                if c:
                    c = c[0].lower() + c[1:]
                stripped_rest.append(c)
            # Join with commas and a final “and”.
            combined = f"{first}, " + ", ".join(stripped_rest[:-1])
            combined += f", and {stripped_rest[-1]}."
            sentences.append(combined)

    # Combine sentences for different subjects with a space.
    sentence = " ".join(sentences)
    # Capitalize first character of the whole output.
    return sentence[0].upper() + sentence[1:]

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END
```
